sentences.text: Infrequent, yes.
Well, we arranged-- first of all, I think we didn't work on Sundays, so this is when we made it that we visited the parents.
My brother came, and I came.
What we talked about, I'm hungry.
I'm hungry.
Is there anything to eat?
And also, you know, I wouldn't say we were not-- we tried to keep everything-- my parents did, you know, not to frighten us because nobody knew what's going to happen.
We lived from one day to the other.
We didn't know what's going to be tomorrow.
We sit together.
And I guess we just sat there, and we talked, and then we went back home again.
And everybody did their own thing.
And-- but there was always so much friction and things going on in Terezin, because she always said, did you hear this one has to come in, and this one has to appear.
This one left.
So it was a constant, you know, who is going to be next?
Who is going to be next?
And this is-- this was the life there.
And in between, like I said, we-- we were mostly kids together, and we tried to make the best out of it.
Once in a while-- once in a while, we heard planes going over, very high over Terezin, and then we said, oh, maybe.
Because we didn't know what was going on outside.
We had no idea.
How come there are planes there?
Maybe these are foreign planes.
Maybe they are-- they're fighting.
Maybe the war's coming to an end.
So there was always that kind of hope.
There was always a hope there.
What stands out in your mind most about your time at Theresienstdat?
Most of the time-- most stands out, first of all, that I was together with my, you know peers, with girls, which I had very good friends there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what can I tell you?
I can only talk about Terezin, how it stands out, if I think about what came afterwards.
While we were there, it was-- there was a constant dying going on.
There were epidemics there.
There where-- people had encephalitis, which is an inflammation of the brain membranes.
People had jaundice, yellow, which I had, too.
So don't forget this proximity of people living together.
One got it, everybody else got it.
There were no medication there, very little.
So whoever got well, got well.
Didn't get well, died.
Especially the older people.
Older people, they died like flies.
And what stands out?
We were still together.
The parents were still-- and we wore regular clothes.
Even though it was old, and it was no good, but it was-- and we had our hair, and we looked like human beings.
That's-- that stands out.
You mentioned you had jaundice at some point?
Yeah.
This was, you know, you your head down, and you got yellow in your face, and your eyes got yellow.
And somehow it was maybe-- it was an epidemic.
And most of them had it, because it's very contagious.
But it somehow went away.
I don't remember that we got some medication because I don't even know if there's anything around for that.
I guess it takes its time.
Either it gets worse, or it gets better.
Again, we really young kids, so maybe we had a little bit more resistance.
There was, yeah.
There were hospitals called [INAUDIBLE].
This was like a Czech name for that, where people got-- but again, how they were treated, if they had medication, I don't know.
I was not in a hospital.
I know friends of mine who had encephalitis.
They had to go to the hospital because they were act like, you know, they were out.
Some died, and some came back.
In Terezin?
Well, the worst thing was, basically, that-- that one day [INAUDIBLE] where we were collected, and we thought we're going to get killed there.
Everybody thought.
They were standing there with the guns just waiting to shoot us.
They're waiting for from Berlin, and we were told they're waiting for something from Berlin what to do with us, because what are we going to do, like 60,000 people standing around there?
What are they going to do with us?
And then like I said, all of a sudden, in the evening, is they're back again, and it was like a horde, all the people.
I don't know how we found our way back all together.
That stands out especially, and of course, saying goodbye to friends who were sent away where we didn't know where they are going.
But it went on constantly.
Yeah, yeah.
I still am, I still am.
She lives very close by here.
She was-- I met her in Judenschule in Vienna, because she lived a different place, and I lived in different place.
And we became best friends when we're 10 years old.
And she also was sent to Terezin, but later, because she got diphtheria in the hospital-- or was it scarlet fever, or whatever-- she got in the camp, so they sent her away.
And her parents and her sister came to Terezin, and she came in January, '93.
But while we were in Vienna, we spend all the time together.
She came to my house, I went to her house.
Went to the cemetery, working there, she was with me.
Always together, always together.
And then when she came to Terezin, we were in the same room, and always best friend.
And then when we went to Auschwitz-- I will tell you that later-- I met her there again.
She lost everybody.
Father, mother, sister, nephew.
Everybody went right the same day into the gas chamber, and by-- by, I don't know, miracle, she walked in in Auschwitz, when we were already in Auschwitz.
And my mother saw her, and says, Gertie, where are your-- she says, I'm all by myself.
So she says, come-- come here, stay here.
They didn't know we were standing in line.
So from that time on, we were always together.
Her name was Gertie Taussig, T-A-U-S-S-I-G.
Now she's Gertie Meltzer.
She used to live in Levittown.
That's the reason that I moved here from Forrest Hills.
And so that I didn't have anybody else here, so we were always very close, and still are.
Well, you couldn't help.
You couldn't help.
Just by talking, just being there, just knowing that there's someone that from before.
And you made other, new friends, but this was something that goes way back, and it still goes way back.

And we still see each other, and we still go together to the opera, so we have kind of a-- it was like people think, most of the people think, we are sisters because she always said that we are sisters.
We didn't want to start telling where are your parents, this one was killed, so we just said we're sisters.
Most of the people here, when they see the tape, they'll find out we are not.
[LAUGHS] I'm not going to show it to anybody who doesn't know.
But that gave her a complete different outlook on life because she was not alone.
And for me, it was good, too.
Well, again, there were constant transport, and one day, my father and my brother were called to go to a different labor camp.
Didn't say what it is.
This was the first transport of men at that particular time, because that-- most of the people were sent away.
This was in September, '44.
And so, we said goodbye to my father, my brother, and then we heard that in the second transport, people, women, can volunteer to go, too, without being called.
And my mother and I, right away.
We went right away, and we said we want to be where my brother and my father is, so we volunteered to go on the second transfer.
That was half women and half men.
Yeah.
While we were sitting with them together-- they were sitting on a heap and waiting to be called to be put into the wagons-- and we were with them.
We said goodbye, and you know, we actually, like I said, my father thought, everybody thought-- they said we are relocating the people to another labor camp, not knowing where they're going.
I mean, we volunteered the next [INAUDIBLE].
We had no idea.
We probably would have been called.
Maybe yes, maybe no.
Maybe we would have stayed back, maybe not, because it was like a lottery.
So when they left, I think like when we volunteered maybe two days later, my mother and I volunteered, and we went also.
They sent us to wherever we got to.
You know, we went into wagons, and-- and looked around.
There was just a little hole on top of the train, and we said, where are we going, where are we going?
Nobody know where we're going.
But then when they opened up the doors, I mean, I just say this was like three days of agony.
There was no food, no bathroom.
I don't know where we went-- wherever we were, we went.
And all of a sudden, they opened the doors, and we saw [GERMAN], and we saw Auschwitz, you know?
That was the station.
And if I see this train things, that gives me the-- it's terrible.
And then they made us jump, you know, jump out there.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], and stand-- you know, stand in line there.
And I remember we had a can of sardines.
My mother had a can of sardines, which she took along.
Sardines which we got once, I don't know when.
It was like gold, you know?
I remember mother opening up the sardines, said Thea, eat, eat, eat, eat.
You know, like this is gold.
Eat it up before-- before they take it away from us.
Right.
And I resisted, but I ate it.
I remember that.
And then we were waiting.
And the people here, you know, they were already Polish prisoners there.
They were with the striped uniforms there.
And [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know?
I mean, yelled at us.
And then we stood in line, and then the selection started, you know?
Again, we didn't know what it is about.
We saw Auschwitz, but we didn't know.
A row of SS, you know, was standing there.
And then I heard in the middle was Mengele, Dr.
Mengele.
He was part of the selecting people.
And then of course, then through a loudspeaker, they said, the camp is far away.
I mean, we were three days in this miserable situation.
The camp is far away.
Old people, young people, people who are sick or are very tired and cannot walk, can be on a-- what do you call it?
A truck.
Can go on the truck, and we take them to the camp.
[SIGHS]
So most of the old people and the people with little children-- that's how Gertie's sister and her little boy got killed.
She was only in her 20s, and she walked in, and-- And then it started.
Left, right, left, right, left, right.
And when they came to my mother, he said, [GERMAN]?
I mean, are you healthy?
She says, I have a cold or something.
My mother was that time 43 years old or something.
So he sent her to the left.
I don't know.
It is a dispute.
I thought it was the right.
People when I hear now, they say left.
I thought they sent her to the right side, and the people to stay alive stayed on the left side.
So he sent my mother that way and me that way, and when I saw all these people going-- all the people.
The people-- even somebody was 40 was old.
And so behind them, I just instinctively grabbed her arm-- grabbed her arm-- and started running this way.
And I said, I don't go where the old people go.
Just instinctively, we walk.
And I started running with her, and that saved her life.
She was already on the other side.
I didn't know that.
This was just instinct.
It was just God made me do it.
I don't know who made me to it.
And-- and then we started walking.
The first thing I saw, people were were not supposed to be outside.
Not even put their head out.
You know, there were these barracks there.
The first thing I saw that a woman put-- looked outside, and somebody shot her in the face.
And I heard that shot, and I saw the blood.
And my mother said, Thea, don't look, don't look, don't look.
So I looked, I saw it, but I kept running.
And then we came-- you know, then we came into a big room, and they said strip.
We had to strip everything completely.
And that was the first thing.
The second thing, we had to sit down and get shaved all over, you know?
Hair and everything, completely.
And then there was a heap of clothing there, probably from people who died or something, and said take a dress and a pair of shoes.
That's all we could take, and that's what that we took.
Funny part is that you didn't-- I didn't recognize hardly-- I didn't recognize anybody.
You know, when you shave the hair off, you don't recognize anybody.
Somebody said, you know, you look good with that.
But you know, it was kind of a-- I don't know, we were just numb.
Numb like an animal, you know?
Like a sheep that got shaved off.
And then we're standing there, and with the clothes, and then we were marched into one of these barracks.
You probably saw it already.
And we got into one of these bunk beds there.
Just there were rows and rows and rows.
There was no mattress on it.
There was no blanket on it.
There was just rows and rows of bunk beds.
There used to be stables, you know, horse stable, those things.
There was in the middle was like like breaks, and left and right.
And there we were, sitting and waiting.
Of course, then they come again, and you had to come off these bunk beds.
And then you have to stand and get undressed, and they selected again.
Anybody who had any kind of sign on the body or any scratches.
People get-- in Theresienstadt, that was the most terrible thing.
They got like infection, like pus-- pus all over.
Young people, especially.
I had a friend, she had it all over.
She didn't survive.
Because when they saw that, boom, that was, of course, already a reason to-- to kill them.
And then, of course, they started the appells.
You had to go, they-- to count you.
To go out and count you early in the morning, like 5:00 in the morning.
Somewhere music played, you know?
They had like a orchestra there.
Played music, and you were standing there shivering.
It was October, and it was in Auschwitz.
It's Silesia.
It's [INAUDIBLE].
It's Silesia.
Cold and muddy.
It was hell.
And again, they counted, and people collapsed there.
And I remember when people collapsed, there were kapos there, Jewish kapos.
I couldn't believe that these are Jewish people, but they were elected to take care of all these people.
So if somebody collapsed, they came with cold water and poured over so that they came to, and they could get up again.
And again, selections and selections, and sleeping there.
We slept like a whole row of people, like the sardines.
And if once turned around, all the rest had to around.
And then once in a while, I mean, we got like a terrible cold thing, something black in the morning to drink.
And we got like pumpkin soup.
It was awful.
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
Like sweet potatoes.
Pumpkin soup once a day.
And this was our existence there.
And selections, and selections, and standing in line.
And it was-- that was until we were sent away to Germany, but that went on for like two, three weeks.
It was October, '44.
It was right after Rosh Hashanah.
October, '44, arrived in Auschwitz.
And then we found out.
Then the people told us already that where are the other people who didn't make it, didn't come with us?
Oh, they are already gassed.
They are already burning.
They are all-- everybody showed to the chimneys, you know.
They were always smoking.
And that's also when Gertie came the next transport, and she stayed with us, so she was with us already together.
So my mother, Gertie, and I, the three of us.
Until one day, they said, again, we are supposed to take a shower.
Now we thought that's it, again, you know?
But it was we took a shower, and we got other clothing.
We got a coat.
Again, we took a coat, we took a dress, and we took a pair of shoes.
There was all heaps of you didn't know.
You grabbed whatever you could.
And I can grabbed a pair of men's shoes, two different kinds, and everybody envied me for those shoes.
Because they were big, you know?
And they were flat.
Because Gertie got high heeled shoes.
She had to walk all this time with the high heel shoes, and it was terrible.
And then we were sent-- we were put it into wagons again, cattle wagons, and didn't know where they're bringing us again.
And we finally arrived in Freiberg, which is Saxony, in a factory.
We had to work in a factory.
And they assembled us there.
It was maybe, I don't know, 200, 300 people there.
Women, only women.
They were not even finished with our barracks.
They we're building barracks.
After a couple of weeks, they took us from the factory to sleep.
This was cold.
There was no heat.
It was still wet, everything, and it was bitter, bitter cold there.
We didn't have underwear or nothing.
I don't even remember how we went to the bathroom, honestly, what we did.
I think we tore out the lining of the coat to clean ourselves.
And this-- this was it, and we were in Freiberg.
Freiberg was a town, a small town, which belonged actually to the concentration camp Flossenburg.
This was the concentration camp, and this was a side camp.
It was a small, peaceful town where people went to work and saw us marching in the morning, and nobody even threw us a piece of bread or anything.
They knew what was going on, and we were [INAUDIBLE] in a factory there.
It was an airplane factory.
They just made wings for airplanes.
I worked on those wings.
I can remember there was a whole bunch of people holding up, and they were soldering, welding their wing down.
I don't know the name for it.
Is is, you know, I don't even think they ever got finished [INAUDIBLE].
But we were holding those things, and somebody was working on it.
And we did this like maybe 15 hours a day.
We marched in the morning to the factory, worked there, and marched back.
Well, the day-- I just know-- I remember that I was always terrible afraid, always like anxious and afraid.
They woke us up like at 5:00 in the morning.
Get up, get up, get up, get up.
Trying to think if we could wash some place, because everything was like ice, ice cold.
I know there was like a latrine there, which when you went there, you had to go out barefoot into the snow, into the latrine, and then back.
And then we put on our dress, and I think the soup we got at the factory.
I mean, the coffee, like drink there, whatever.
And a piece of bread that we kept until, you know, either ate it right away-- it's just once a day we got it.
So we had to line up, and we lined up.
We had to walk to the factory quite a-- quite a distance.
I know we walked a little faster.
Gertie and I, I remember, we walked faster.
And you know what it is, when you walk in the snow, it built up on your shoe.
And all of a sudden, we had like we were so high because it was like wet snow, and it built up on your shoes.
And we turned around, and we didn't see anybody.
I mean, we walked too fast.
So we were standing there waiting for the other people to catch up with us because we had no place to go.
And in the factory, we started-- and there was like a very bad supervisor, SS guy, and a lot of SS women, supervisor with that gray uniform, with the whip, with dogs, you know.
And so, they frightened us tremendously.
Tremendously.
And that was our-- were our days.
There were a lot of bed box we had.
We each got one blanket, and we-- there was a straw mattress, just a straw.
So I slept on one small bed together with my mother.
So that was a kind of good because we slept one head was here and one head here, and we warmed our feet this way.
You know, I held her feet, she held mine, so we could.
But people who slept with strangers, you know, they put themselves in a blanket and the other one in a blanket.
And these were our days in Freiberg, Freiberg in Saxony.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
I remember that when we walked once, we walked in-- my mother walked into a door where she was not supposed to.
I don't remember if it said something on top, but she walked in there.
And this-- this SS guard came, and he hit her so badly.
Just this, you know.
And you're not supposed to go through this door, and he really hit her bad.
She was bleeding.
But that-- that was in my memory.
And the women were very bad, too, you know.
Any time you walked where you're not supposed to, they let the dog loose on you.
So it was pretty bad.
No way, no way, no way.
Nobody-- we worked with non-Jewish Germans.
Don't forget it was 1944, and they took the younger one, already, into the war.
They needed every man [INAUDIBLE].
So there were older guys there.
Not one-- not one gave us anything, or even told us.
Maybe they were afraid, but they could have told us that the war's coming closer, and it's going to go to an end, or give us any kind of hope.
Nobody did.
Nobody did.
The only thing that we remember that was-- was also when you asked me what February 15, 1945.
See, there were air raids constantly there, and they locked us on the top floor of the factory.
So if the factory would have been hit, we would be the first one.
That was my other-- I was so afraid.
I was always telling my mother, what is if we are not dead right away?
What is if-- if we are just wounded?
They'll send us back to Auschwitz and gas us.
So this-- this is what I mean being afraid.
And then also in the factory one time, you know, I had like oil here from whatever we worked, and I rubbed it.
And my hand got inflamed.
I got metal poisoning.
My arm was from here to here I couldn't feel it, and it was just hanging there.
And there was no medication.
There was nothing there.
And I didn't know what to do, but the one day, I don't know, this thing opened up, and it started draining and draining.
And again, this was my luck.
I mean, it could have been, it could have not been.
I could have been completely poisoned.
So that got better, and then in February-- in February, 1945, they had the big air raid on Dresden.
I think people know about it.
And we were-- we saw everything.
We were between Chemnitz and Dresden.
OK.
We'll stop tape number three here and pick this up with tape number four.
OK.
